The course covers a wide range of measuring instruments for levels, pressure, flow, temperature, and chemical composition, alongside essential concepts in process control and data analysis. Students will gain a foundational understanding of instrumentation diagrams, analytical techniques like gas chromatography and mass spectrometry, and the electrical principles underpinning many measurement devices, preparing them for practical application in the chemical process industries.

outline.title: Introduction to Process Instrumentation & Measurement Fundamentals
subtopics: Analyze the fundamental principles of process measurement, including accuracy, precision, resolution, and sensitivity, and evaluate their impact on overall process control system performance.

Differentiate between various types of sensors and transducers commonly used in chemical engineering processes, specifically relating their operating principles to the measurement of process variables like temperature, pressure, flow, and level.
Implement signal conditioning techniques, such as amplification, filtering, and linearization, to optimize sensor signals for accurate data acquisition and control system integration.

Design a basic process instrumentation loop, selecting appropriate sensors, transmitters, controllers, and final control elements to maintain a specified process variable within a defined operating range.
Evaluate the sources of error in process measurements and apply appropriate calibration methods to minimize uncertainty and ensure the reliability of instrumentation systems.

outline.title: Level and Pressure Measurement
subtopics: Analyze the operating principles, advantages, and disadvantages of various level measurement techniques including differential pressure, ultrasonic, radar, and capacitance, and select the most appropriate technique for a given industrial process scenario considering factors like fluid properties, vessel geometry, and cost.

Evaluate the sources of error and uncertainty associated with different pressure measurement devices such as Bourdon tubes, diaphragms, and strain gauges, and implement calibration procedures to minimize measurement inaccuracies within acceptable tolerances.

Design a level measurement system for a chemical process unit, specifying the necessary instrumentation, signal conditioning, and control logic to maintain a desired liquid level setpoint while accounting for process dynamics and potential disturbances.

Compare and contrast the application of static and dynamic pressure measurements in process control, and determine the appropriate pressure tapping locations and instrumentation for accurate flow rate determination using differential pressure flow meters.

Implement troubleshooting strategies for common issues encountered with level and pressure transmitters, including signal loss, drift, and sensor failure, and interpret diagnostic data to identify root causes and recommend corrective actions.

outline.title: Flow Measurement Techniques
subtopics: Analyze the principles of operation, advantages, and disadvantages of various flow measurement devices including differential pressure flow meters (e.g., orifice plates, venturi meters, flow nozzles), positive displacement meters, and turbine meters, quantifying their suitability for specific industrial applications based on fluid properties and process conditions.

Evaluate the impact of fluid properties (density, viscosity, compressibility) and installation effects on the accuracy of flow measurement techniques, calculating correction factors and assessing the associated uncertainties in flow rate determination.

Design a flow measurement system for a given chemical process, selecting appropriate instrumentation and specifying necessary piping configurations to minimize errors and ensure reliable flow rate measurement within defined process constraints.

Implement and interpret calibration procedures for flow meters, utilizing traceable standards and applying statistical methods to determine meter performance characteristics and establish appropriate maintenance schedules.

Compare and contrast the operational principles and applications of different flow rate measurement technologies, such as ultrasonic flow meters, Coriolis flow meters, and thermal mass flow meters, justifying the selection of a specific technology based on cost, accuracy, and process compatibility.

outline.title: Temperature Measurement and Control
subtopics: Analyze the principles of operation, advantages, and disadvantages of various temperature sensors including thermocouples, RTDs, and thermistors, and select the most appropriate sensor for a given chemical engineering process application considering factors like temperature range, accuracy, and response time.

Evaluate the impact of lead resistance and compensation techniques on the accuracy of thermocouple measurements, and implement appropriate correction methods to minimize measurement errors in industrial settings.

Design and interpret temperature measurement schemes incorporating concepts of radiation, conduction, and convection to accurately determine the temperature of fluids and surfaces within chemical process equipment.
Implement signal conditioning techniques, such as cold junction compensation and linearization, to improve the reliability and precision of temperature signals for process control applications.

Compare and contrast different temperature control strategies, including on-off control, proportional control, and PID control, and determine the optimal control parameters to maintain a desired temperature setpoint while minimizing overshoot and settling time in a simulated process.

outline.title: Chemical Composition Analysis: Fundamentals
subtopics: Apply stoichiometric principles and chemical reaction equations to quantitatively analyze the composition of reacting systems, calculating limiting reactants, percent conversion, and theoretical/actual yields with at least 80% accuracy.

Evaluate and compare different analytical techniques for determining chemical composition, including gravimetric analysis, titrimetry, and spectroscopic methods, identifying the appropriate technique based on sample properties and desired precision.

Design and implement a process for determining the concentration of a specific component in a multi-component mixture using principles of mass balance and appropriate analytical instrumentation, documenting the procedure and potential sources of error.

Analyze experimental data obtained from chemical composition analysis, utilizing statistical methods to assess data quality, calculate confidence intervals, and determine the significance of observed variations.

Interpret process instrumentation signals related to chemical composition, such as pH, conductivity, and concentration measurements, to evaluate process performance and implement control strategies for maintaining desired product quality within specified tolerances.

outline.title: Gas Chromatography (GC) Principles and Applications
subtopics: Analyze the fundamental principles of gas chromatography, including stationary and mobile phase interactions, to predict the separation of different chemical species based on their physical and chemical properties.

Evaluate the impact of key GC parameters – such as column type, temperature programming, and carrier gas flow rate – on resolution, retention time, and peak shape, justifying parameter selections for specific separation challenges.

Implement qualitative and quantitative analysis techniques using GC data, including peak identification, calibration curve generation, and determination of component concentrations in complex mixtures.

Design a GC experimental setup, selecting appropriate detectors (e.g., FID, TCD, MS) and columns based on the target analytes and desired sensitivity, while considering safety protocols and instrument limitations.

Differentiate between various GC applications in chemical engineering, such as process monitoring, quality control, and environmental analysis, and assess the advantages and disadvantages of GC compared to other analytical techniques for each application.

outline.title: Mass Spectrometry (MS) Fundamentals
subtopics: Analyze the fundamental principles of mass spectrometry, including ionization methods (e.g., electron ionization, chemical ionization, electrospray ionization) and mass analyzers (e.g., quadrupole, time-of-flight, ion trap) to predict the fragmentation patterns of simple organic molecules.

Evaluate the impact of different mass spectrometer components on resolution, sensitivity, and mass accuracy, and select appropriate instrument configurations for specific analytical challenges in chemical engineering process monitoring and control.

Implement data interpretation techniques for mass spectra, including identifying molecular ions, isotopic peaks, and characteristic fragment ions, to determine the molecular weight and structural information of unknown compounds.

Design a qualitative analysis workflow utilizing mass spectrometry to identify and quantify key components in a complex mixture relevant to chemical process streams, considering potential interferences and matrix effects.

Compare and contrast the advantages and limitations of various mass spectrometry techniques (e.g., GC-MS, LC-MS, ICP-MS) for applications in process analytical chemistry, specifically relating their capabilities to the measurement and control of chemical processes.

outline.title: Sampling Systems and Process Instrumentation Diagrams (PIDs)
subtopics: Analyze the components of a representative sampling system, including sample taps, transport lines, and analytical instruments, to determine potential sources of error and propose mitigation strategies for accurate process monitoring.

Design a Process Instrumentation Diagram (PID) for a specified chemical process unit, accurately depicting control loops, instrumentation symbols, and process equipment, adhering to industry standards and demonstrating understanding of signal flow.

Evaluate the selection criteria for different types of process analyzers (e.g., gas chromatographs, pH meters, spectrophotometers) based on process requirements such as accuracy, response time, and cost, justifying the chosen analyzer for a given application.
Implement a systematic approach to interpreting PIDs, identifying key process variables, control strategies, and safety interlocks, and predicting system behavior under various operating conditions.

Differentiate between various sampling techniques (e.g., grab sampling, continuous sampling, composite sampling) and assess their suitability for different process streams and analytical requirements, considering factors like homogeneity and representativeness.

outline.title: Electrical Components and Circuits for Instrumentation
subtopics: Analyze basic electrical circuits (series, parallel, series-parallel) commonly used in instrumentation systems, calculating voltage, current, and resistance using Ohm’s Law and Kirchhoff’s Laws with at least 80% accuracy on quantitative problems.

Evaluate the characteristics of common electrical components – resistors, capacitors, inductors, diodes, and transistors – and select appropriate components for specific instrumentation applications, justifying component choices based on performance specifications.

Design and implement signal conditioning circuits, including voltage dividers, amplifiers, and filters, to convert sensor outputs into usable signals for control systems, demonstrating functionality through simulation or breadboard prototyping.

Explain the principles of grounding and shielding techniques to minimize electrical noise and interference in instrumentation systems, and assess the impact of improper grounding on signal accuracy and system reliability.

Troubleshoot basic electrical faults in instrumentation circuits using appropriate test equipment (multimeters, oscilloscopes) and interpret circuit diagrams to identify and resolve component failures or wiring errors with a documented troubleshooting process.

outline.title: Advanced Sensors: Humidity, Density, Viscosity, and pH
subtopics: Analyze the operating principles of capacitive, resistive, and optical humidity sensors, evaluating their suitability for specific process control applications based on accuracy, response time, and environmental limitations.

Design a density measurement system utilizing differential pressure transmitters or vibrating element densitometers, calculating expected performance characteristics given fluid properties and process conditions.

Implement signal conditioning techniques for viscosity sensors (e.g., rotameters, capillary viscometers) to improve measurement accuracy and compensate for temperature variations, demonstrating understanding of relevant calibration procedures.

Evaluate the impact of temperature and ionic strength on pH measurements, selecting appropriate pH electrodes and buffer solutions for accurate process monitoring and control in chemical engineering systems.

Compare and contrast the advantages and disadvantages of various advanced sensors (humidity, density, viscosity, pH) in terms of cost, maintenance, and integration with distributed control systems (DCS), justifying sensor selection for a given chemical process scenario.

outline.title: Fluidisation and Particle Technology
subtopics: Apply fundamental principles of fluid dynamics and particle mechanics to analyze the behavior of fluidized beds, predicting minimum fluidization velocity and bed expansion ratio for given particle and fluid properties.

Evaluate the performance characteristics of different types of fluidization regimes (e.g., bubbling, slugging, turbulent, fast fluidization) based on operating conditions and physical properties, justifying the selection of an appropriate regime for a specific chemical engineering process.

Design a fluidized bed reactor for a given chemical reaction, considering factors such as particle size distribution, gas velocity, heat transfer requirements, and pressure drop, and assessing its potential advantages and disadvantages compared to other reactor types.

Implement and interpret techniques for characterizing particle properties relevant to fluidization, including particle size analysis, shape factor determination, and density measurement, relating these properties to fluidization behavior.

Assess the impact of fluidization on mass transfer and reaction rates in chemical processes, utilizing relevant correlations and models to predict performance and troubleshoot potential operational issues related to particle attrition, elutriation, and backmixing.

outline.title: Process Control and Reactor Design Considerations
subtopics: Analyze the dynamic behavior of chemical processes, including first and second-order systems, utilizing transfer function representations and evaluating time-domain specifications like settling time and overshoot to assess system stability and performance.

Design and implement Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controllers for single-loop control systems, selecting appropriate controller parameters based on process characteristics and performance objectives, and predicting the impact of parameter tuning on system response.

Evaluate the impact of reactor dynamics, including residence time distribution (RTD) and non-ideal flow patterns, on reactor performance metrics such as conversion and selectivity, applying concepts from C&R Chemical Engineering Solutions to solve related problems.

Apply fundamental principles of process instrumentation to select appropriate sensors and transmitters for measuring key process variables (e.g., temperature, pressure, flow) and integrate them into a control system architecture, considering accuracy, range, and reliability.

Differentiate between various reactor design considerations, such as batch, continuous stirred-tank (CSTR), and plug flow reactors (PFR), and analyze their respective control challenges, utilizing unit operations principles to optimize reactor operation and stability.

title: OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
description: COE 302 explores object-oriented programming principles using Python. The course covers inheritance and class hierarchies, enabling the creation of reusable and organized code. Students will learn to define classes, methods, and utilize inheritance to build complex systems.

title: ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS AND DATA STRUCTURES
NUMERICAL COMPUTATIONS
description: COE 405 covers fundamental concepts in numerical analysis, focusing on methods for solving ordinary and partial differential equations. The course emphasizes mathematical principles alongside practical application using software like MATLAB, preparing students for advanced engineering problem-solving. It aims to transform undergraduate STEM education through numerical methods.

title: PROTOTYPING TECHNIQUES
description: COE 409 provides a comprehensive introduction to electronic prototyping, covering essential steps from initial concept to final packaging. This course emphasizes the importance of prototyping for design verification, iterative development, and risk reduction in the creation of electronic systems. Students will gain practical knowledge of prototyping techniques, simulation software, and PCB design.

title: OPERATING SYSTEM
description: COE 413 provides a comprehensive introduction to the principles of operating systems. The course covers fundamental concepts like process management, memory management, and file systems, alongside practical considerations like scheduling algorithms and inter-process communication. Students will gain an understanding of both the theoretical foundations and practical implementations of modern operating systems.

title: DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKS
description: COE 427 provides an introduction to data communications and networks, covering fundamental concepts from data representation to transmission techniques. The course explores the components of communication systems and examines various methods for encoding and transmitting data, preparing students for advanced study in the field. It also touches upon error detection and correction in digital data communication.

title: CONTROL THEORY AND SYSTEMS
description: COE 441 explores the fundamentals of data communication and networking, covering topics from network structures and models to error detection and correction. The course delves into layered tasks and protocols essential for reliable data transmission and network operation.

title: ARTIFICIAL NEUTRAL NETWORK
description: COE 501 explores natural language processing techniques using Python, machine learning, and deep learning. This course focuses on unlocking insights from text data through practical recipes and real-world applications. Students will learn to extract, process, and analyze textual information effectively.

title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
description: This course, COE 502, provides an introduction to the field of Artificial Intelligence, suitable for computer science students and those with an interest in the subject. It covers a broad range of AI topics, from historical foundations to modern techniques like machine learning and planning. The book assumes some familiarity with algorithms and data structures.

outline.title: Expert Systems
subtopics: Inference and Resolution for Problem Solving

title: COMPUTER SECURITY TECNIQUES
description: COE 503 provides a foundational understanding of computer security, covering confidentiality, integrity, and availability. The course explores various attacks, security services, and methods for preventing internal and external threats, including malware and social engineering. Students will also learn to perform risk assessments and develop security policies.

title: EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
description: COE 504 explores the design and implementation of embedded systems, emphasizing functional partitioning and a systematic methodology for development. The course covers both hardware and software aspects, progressing from high-level system design to detailed component implementation and testing. Students will learn to decompose complex systems into manageable sub-functions and utilize appropriate building blocks.

title: SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
description: COE 505 explores the systematic and disciplined approach to software development, encompassing its lifecycle from requirements to maintenance. The course covers both traditional software life cycle models and modern agile methodologies, emphasizing the importance of quality, efficiency, and adaptability in software engineering practices. Students will learn about testing strategies and coding guidelines to produce robust and maintainable software.

title: MODELLING AND SIMULATION OF COMPUTER SYSTEMS
description: COE 507 introduces students to Computer Aided Design, focusing on project and document management within an Altium Designer environment. The course covers creating, modifying, and organizing projects, as well as utilizing various view commands for effective design visualization. Students will learn to work with schematic and PCB documents, and understand the role of libraries in the design process.

title: COMPUTER GRAPHICS AND ANIMATION
description: COE 508 explores the fundamental principles of 3D modeling techniques used in computer-aided design. This course covers wireframe, surface, and solid modeling, focusing on their advantages, disadvantages, construction methods, and applications in creating and manipulating digital representations of physical objects. Students will learn how these models support design, visualization, and analysis.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MOBILE COMPUTING
description: COE 520 provides an introduction to mobile computing, exploring its applications, challenges, and underlying technologies. The course covers wireless media, network standards, and current trends in mobile communications, preparing students to design and implement wireless networks based on user requirements. It also examines the evolution of cellular systems and emerging technologies like WiMAX and LTE.

title: DESIGN AND INSTALLATION OF ELECTRICAL DEVICES AND ICT
INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
ELECTRONICS I
description: EEE 313 provides a foundational understanding of electronic devices and circuits, covering both semiconductor theory and practical applications. The course explores diodes, transistors, and basic amplifier configurations, preparing students for more advanced topics in analog and digital electronics. Emphasis is placed on circuit analysis, modeling, and design principles.

title: ELECTRONICS II (ANALOGUE)
description: EEE 314 covers fundamental electronic devices and circuits, focusing on both discrete components and basic digital logic. The course explores amplifier configurations, operational amplifier applications, and sequential logic circuits like flip-flops and counters. It provides a foundation for analyzing and designing electronic systems.

title: ELECTROMAGNETIC FIELD AND WAVES I
description: EEE 315 explores the fundamentals of electromagnetic fields and waves, focusing on electrostatic principles and their application to various charge distributions. The course covers key concepts like Coulomb's Law, electric field intensity, and Gauss's Law, alongside practical problem-solving related to force calculations and field determination. It builds a foundation for understanding electromagnetic phenomena and their behavior in different scenarios.

title: ELECTROMAGNETIC FIELD AND WAVE THEORY II
MEASUREMENT AND INSTRUMENTATION
description: EEE 319 (Measurement & Instrumentation) provides a comprehensive study of electrical and electronic measurement techniques. The course covers fundamental units, error theory, and various indicating instruments, alongside modern digital test equipment and signal conditioning methods. It aims to equip students with the knowledge to select, use, and understand the limitations of measurement systems.

title: ELECTRIC CIRCUIT THEORY I
description: EEE 321 Electric Circuit Theory 1 provides a foundational understanding of electrical networks and circuit analysis techniques. This course covers essential concepts like circuit laws, network classification, and methods for solving complex circuits using both mesh and nodal analysis. Students will learn to analyze both linear and non-linear circuits, and understand the behavior of various circuit elements.

title: ELECTRIC CIRCUIT THEORY II
description: EEE 322 delves into the analysis of circuits using Fourier series and transforms, alongside network function concepts. The course covers signal representation, spectral analysis, and the relationship between time and frequency domains, essential for understanding circuit behavior. It also touches upon non-linear systems and impedance concepts.

title: LINEAR SYSTEMS
description: EEE 324 (Linear Systems) focuses on the analysis and design of control systems through mathematical modeling of physical systems. The course covers analogues concepts across various engineering domains and explores both open-loop and closed-loop system configurations. Students will learn techniques for assessing system stability and understanding frequency response characteristics.

outline.title: Transfer Functions
subtopics: Block diagrams and signal flow graphs

title: ELECTRICAL MACHINES I
description: EEE 325, Electrical Machines I, provides a foundational understanding of electromechanical energy conversion and its applications. The course covers the construction, operating principles, and performance characteristics of DC machines, generators, and motors, preparing students for more advanced studies in electrical machinery. Emphasis is placed on practical problem-solving and analysis of electrical machine systems.

title: SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT TECHNIQUES
description: EEE 341 provides a foundational understanding of C++ programming, covering both its structure and object-oriented principles. The course progresses from basic program construction and input/output operations to more advanced concepts like classes, objects, inheritance, and polymorphism, preparing students for practical application in software development.

title: NUMERICAL COMPUTATIONS
description: EEE 405 provides a comprehensive introduction to numerical methods and their applications in various engineering disciplines. The course covers error analysis, equation solving, interpolation, numerical integration, and ordinary differential equations, emphasizing practical problem-solving techniques. Students will learn to apply these methods to real-world problems across diverse engineering fields.

title: ASSEMBLY LANGUAGE PROGRAMMING
description: EEE 415 provides a comprehensive introduction to assembly language programming for x86 processors. The course covers fundamental concepts, microprocessor architecture, and practical programming techniques, culminating in the ability to write and debug assembly language programs. Students will learn to utilize Irvine32 for program development.

title: DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKS
description: EEE 427 provides a comprehensive study of data communications and networking fundamentals. The course covers network models, data transmission techniques, and essential protocols, preparing students to analyze and design various network architectures. Emphasis is placed on understanding security issues and services within the context of modern data networks.

title: ELECTRIC POWER PRINCIPLES
description: EEE 431 focuses on the economic operation of power systems, covering generation, transmission, and distribution. The course emphasizes optimizing power system performance by matching load with available generation while minimizing costs, considering various generator types and their associated economic factors. It also introduces fundamental concepts of power system structure and polyphase systems.

title: CONTROL THEORY AND SERVOMECHANISM
description: EEE 441 (Control Systems & Servomechanism) provides a comprehensive study of control systems, encompassing linear system analysis, mathematical modeling, and stability analysis. The course explores various control system components like servomechanisms, and delves into advanced techniques such as state-space representation and root locus analysis.

title: DIGITAL ELECTRONICS AND SYSTEMS
description: EEE 471 is a 400-level course in Digital Electronics and Systems, covering the representation and manipulation of digital data. The course explores fundamental concepts like Boolean algebra and logic gates, culminating in the design and programming of microcontrollers and simple CPUs. Students will gain practical experience through tutorials utilizing platforms like Arduino.

title: DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN WITH VDHL PROGRAMMING
MODELLING AND SIMULATION OF ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
description: EEE 507 explores the principles and practices of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) and simulations essential for electrical and computer engineering. The course covers modeling, simulation techniques, schematic capture, PCB design, and analysis, preparing students for practical applications in the field. Emphasis is placed on utilizing software tools for design and verification.

title: ADVANCED ELECTRONICS
description: EEE 509, Advanced Electronics, covers the fundamentals of linear and digital integrated circuits. The course explores both linear IC operations, including op-amps and converters, and digital-analog interfacing, alongside memory circuits and timer ICs. It provides a foundation for understanding and designing advanced electronic systems.

title: DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
description: EEE 511 provides a comprehensive introduction to digital signal processing, covering both theory and practical implementation. The course explores signal representation, system analysis, filter design, and essential transforms for processing signals in the digital domain. It emphasizes the conversion from analog to digital signals and their subsequent manipulation.

title: DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
description: EEE 512 explores the fundamentals of electrical communication systems, focusing on transmitting messages from a source to a destination with high rate and accuracy. The course examines the functional elements of both analog and digital systems, including encoding, modulation, and the impact of channel noise. It also introduces concepts related to probability and joint events within communication systems.

title: RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
description: EEE 514 focuses on the principles and practical application of reliability engineering, covering failure analysis, system assessment, and design for improved reliability. The course explores reliability concepts within power systems and electronic components, utilizing standards like MIL-HDBK-217F to assess component failures and enhance system performance.

title: POWER SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
description: EEE 553/554 provides a comprehensive study of power systems engineering, covering analysis, control, and protection. The course delves into load flow studies, fault analysis, and stability, preparing students for advanced work in power system planning and operation. It builds a foundation for understanding modern power system communication and protection schemes.

title: HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
description: EEE 555 provides a comprehensive overview of high voltage engineering principles and practices, covering the generation, transmission, and utilization of high voltages in power systems and other applications. The course explores insulating materials, testing methodologies, and overvoltage phenomena crucial for ensuring reliable and safe operation of high voltage equipment.

outline.title: High Voltage Insulating Materials: Solids & Liquids

title: CONTROL ENGINEERING
description: EEE 561 is an introductory course to non-linear systems, covering both analysis and control techniques. The course explores state-space representations, stability analysis, and control design methods for linear and non-linear systems, with applications in areas like robotics and circuit synthesis. It also delves into observer design and optimal control strategies.

title: SEMINARS ON USE OF ENGINEERING SOFTWARE PACKAGES IN ELECTRICAL/ELECTRONICS FIELD
CONTROL ENGINEERING III (NON-LINEAR AND PROCESS AUTOMATION)
description: EEE 566 provides an introduction to the analysis and control of nonlinear systems, a crucial aspect of many engineering applications. The course covers methods for approximating nonlinear systems with linear models and explores stability analysis techniques, including phase-plane methods and Lyapunov's indirect method. It also introduces the fundamentals of sampled-data systems within the context of nonlinear control.

title: LEAN PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT AND INDUSTRIAL LOGISTICS
COMPUTER APPLICATIONS (CAD/CAM) IN DESIGN AND MANUFACTURE
INTRODUCTION TO PETROLEUM AND GAS ENGINEERING
